parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: My chest gets this tight, heavy feeling that just sits right behind my ribs. I used to just shrug it off and keep going with whatever I was doing, but lately it lingers longer than it should. It feels like a rubber band slowly stretching around my lungs, making every breath feel a little shallow. I try to ignore it while I sit on the couch, but the sensation just keeps pulling my attention inward. It is honestly exhausting to fight through this weird pressure when I just want to relax.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really uncomfortable and draining. A tight, heavy feeling in the chest can be hard to ignore, especially when it keeps pulling your attention inward and makes breathing feel more effortful.

I can’t tell from this whether it’s anxiety, muscle tension, something reflux-related, or something medical that needs attention — but chest pressure that’s lingering or changing deserves care. If this is new, getting worse, or happening along with shortness of breath, dizziness, sweating, nausea, pain spreading to your arm/jaw/back, or if you feel faint, please seek urgent medical help right away.

If you’re safe right now, a few things that may help in the moment:
- Unclench your jaw and shoulders
- Try slower breathing: inhale gently through your nose for 4, exhale for 6
- Notice whether the pressure changes with position, movement, or after eating
- Sip some water and step away from anything stressful for a minute

If you want, I can stay with you and help you figure out whether this feels more physical, anxiety-related, or both.

turns.user_message: That same tightness in my chest has become a constant background noise that I cannot seem to shake. It used to be a fleeting thing, but now it shows up almost every single evening without fail. I catch myself holding my breath without even realizing it, waiting for the pressure to finally ease up. My shoulders stay locked up near my ears and my hands get clammy even when the room is cool. I keep telling myself it will pass, but the heavy weight just refuses to lift and it drains all my energy.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really exhausting and scary to carry around every evening. The way you describe it—tight chest, shallow breathing, tense shoulders, clammy hands—really sounds like your body is staying in a constant state of alarm, even when you’re trying to rest.

A few things that may help in the moment:

- **Exhale longer than you inhale.** Try breathing in for 4, out for 6 or 8, for a few minutes.
- **Drop your shoulders on purpose.** Let them rise with an inhale, then fully release them on the exhale.
- **Unclench the hands/jaw.** It sounds small, but those areas often hold a lot of tension.
- **Grounding:** name 5 things you see, 4 you feel, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
- **Warmth or movement:** a warm shower, a short walk, or stretching can help tell your body it’s safe.

Because this is happening **almost every evening** and involves chest tightness and breath-holding, it would be a good idea to **check in with a medical professional** as well, just to make sure there isn’t a physical cause contributing to it. If you ever have chest pain that’s severe, new, spreading, or comes with trouble breathing, dizziness, fainting, or pain in your arm/jaw, seek urgent help right away.

If you want, I can help you figure out:
1. what tends to trigger it in the evenings, or  
2. a quick 5-minute reset routine you can try tonight.

turns.user_message: The heavy pressure in my chest has completely taken over my entire body and I feel completely trapped inside my own skin. What started as a minor annoyance has turned into this relentless wave that crashes over me whenever I try to settle down. My heart starts pounding so hard it echoes in my ears, and my legs feel like they are made of wet cement. I pace around the house trying to shake off the suffocating grip, but the sensation just tightens its hold. I am so tired of feeling like I am constantly bracing for impact when nothing is even happening.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really overwhelming and exhausting. What you’re describing can feel terrifying, especially when your body seems to stay on high alert even when nothing immediate is happening.

A few things you can try right now, just to help your body get a little signal of safety:

- **Plant both feet on the floor** and press them down gently.
- **Lengthen your exhale**: inhale for 4, exhale for 6–8, a few times.
- **Name 5 things you can see** and 4 things you can feel around you.
- If pacing is helping even a little, keep it slow and intentional rather than fighting the sensation.
- Sip something cool or hold a cold object in your hands.

If you can, remind yourself: **“This is a body alarm. It feels real, but it is not the same as danger.”**

If the chest pressure is new, severe, or comes with trouble breathing, dizziness, fainting, or pain spreading to your arm/jaw, please get urgent medical help right away, since it’s important to rule out physical causes.

If you want, I can stay with you and help you through a 1-minute grounding exercise right now.
